data.title: Shoe Carnival GAAP EPS of $0.34, revenue of $277.72M
data.description:
• Shoe Carnival press release (NASDAQ:SCVL): Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.34.
  • Revenue of $277.72M (-7.5% Y/Y).
  • Comparable stores net sales declined 8.1 percent, of which the Company estimates approximately 1 percent was due to lost sales as impacted by the rebanner strategy.

  • Based on first quarter EPS exceeding market expectations, rebanner strategy momentum, and some improvement in macroeconomic uncertainties, the Company reaffirmed its entire Fiscal 2025 outlook and continues to expect the following:

    • Net Sales: $1.15 billion to $1.23 billion, representing a range of down 4 percent to up 2 percent versus Fiscal 2024.
    • GAAP EPS: $1.60 to $2.10, inclusive of the rebanner strategy's initial year costs.
    • Gross Profit Margin: 35 percent to 36 percent.
    • SG&A: $350 million to $360 million.
    • Capital Expenditures: $45 to $60 million.

data.title: Qudian reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Qudian press release (NYSE:QD): Q1 total revenues were RMB25.8 million (US$3.6 million), compared to RMB55.8 million for the same period of last year.
  • EPADS of $0.12.

data.title: Commodity Roundup: Gold set for fifth monthly gain amid global tensions; base metals slip
data.description:
Gold prices were on course for their fifth monthly gain on Friday, buoyed by persistent economic and geopolitical tensions, while base metals eased amid fading US tariff optimism.

Spot gold (XAUUSD:CUR) was trading around $3,296.98 an ounce at the time of writing, up over 2% for the month.

The safe-haven metal, however, faced a weekly fall as investors turned cautious ahead of the US PCE inflation report that could provide fresh insights on the Federal Reserve’s interest rate trajectory.

Gold typically benefits from lower interest rates as it reduces the opportunity cost of holding the non-yielding asset.

Saxo Bank's Ole Hansen, in a recent note, stated that gold has settled into a relatively narrow, but downward-trending range as it consolidates after several months of strong performance.

"Currently, gold is lacking a clear directional trend, primarily due to conflicting macroeconomic signals and investor hesitation. Markets are waiting to assess the full economic impact of Trump’s shifting trade policies."

Most analysts, he said, expect the broader effects to be negative, which could prove supportive for gold, particularly under the looming risk of stagflation—a scenario characterized by slowing growth combined with rising inflation.

Although upcoming U.S. trade agreements—especially with major partners in Europe and Asia—could help mitigate some of the adverse economic effects and reduce demand for traditional safe-haven assets like precious metals, persistent concerns remain, Hansen noted.

"Chief among them is the widening U.S. budget deficit, which raises alarms about long-term fiscal sustainability."

In Saxo Bank's view, this, combined with stagflation risks and continued central bank demand, will likely continue to support investment interest in gold and other precious metals such as silver (XAGUSD:CUR) and platinum (XPTUSD:CUR).

Elsewhere, industrial metals declined on Friday as the dollar strengthened, and market optimism waned following a court decision to reinstate the broadest tariffs imposed by U.S. President Donald Trump.

In other London metals, aluminium (LMAHDS03:COM), zinc (LMZSDS03:COM), lead (LL1:COM), nickel (LN1:COM) and tin (LMSNDS03:COM) dipped. Copper (HG1:COM) slipped 3.7% this week.

ETFs liked to precious metals: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (GDX),(GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ),(SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Nvidia, Dell to provide new supercomputer to US Department of Energy
data.description:
The U.S. Department of Energy said on Thursday that its new supercomputer Doudna will use technologies from Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Dell Technologies (NYSE:DELL).

The U.S. Secretary of Energy Chris Wright announced a new contract with Dell to develop NERSC-10, the next flagship supercomputer at the National Energy Research Scientific Computing Center, or NERSC, a DOE user facility at the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory.

The new system, due in 2026, will be named after Jennifer Doudna, the Berkeley Lab-based biochemist who was awarded the 2020 Nobel Prize for Chemistry for her work on gene-editing technology CRISPR.

The DOE said the Dell system will be powered by Nvidia's next-generation Vera Rubin platform and will help in large-scale high-performance computing, or HPC, workloads like those in molecular dynamics, high-energy physics, and AI training and inference.

“Doudna is a time machine for science — compressing years of discovery into days,” said Nvidia Founder and CEO Jensen Huang.

The DOE noted that Doudna will be one of the most advanced supercomputers ever deployed by it.

"It will be a powerhouse for rapid innovation that will transform our efforts to develop abundant, affordable energy supplies and advance breakthroughs in quantum computing. AI is the Manhattan Project of our time, and Doudna will help ensure America’s scientists have the tools they need to win the global race for AI dominance," said Wright.

NERSC Director Sudip Dosanjh noted that Doudna will be connected to DOE experimental facilities via the Energy Sciences Network, or ESnet, allowing scientists to stream data into the system from across the country and analyze it in near-real time.

data.title: SEC ends legal battle with top crypto exchange Binance
data.description:
The Securities and Exchange Commission has dropped its lawsuit against Binance and its founder Changpeng Zhao, bringing an end to the two-year legal battle with the world's largest cryptocurrency exchange.

A joint stipulation to dismiss the lawsuit was submitted by the SEC, Binance and Zhao. In the court filing, the SEC said dismissing the lawsuit was appropriate "in the exercise of its discretion and as a policy matter."

The parties sought for the civil enforcement action to be dismissed with prejudice, meaning the SEC cannot pursue this case again. 

Binance said the dismissal is a "huge win" for crypto. "U.S. innovation is back on track - and it's just the beginning," it posted on X.

The SEC filed the lawsuit against Binance in June 2023, accusing it of artificially inflating trading volumes, commingling customer funds and unlawfully enabling trade of cryptos it viewed as unregistered securities at the time.

The lawsuit was separate from Binance's guilty plea and $4.3B penalty in 2023 for allegedly violating U.S. anti-money laundering rules, for which Zhao served a four-month prison sentence.

The dismissal of the SEC's enforcement action against Binance is viewed as a symbolic end to the Biden-era regulatory crackdown on the crypto industry.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: MRVL, PATH, GAP, and more
data.description:
Stock futures dipped slightly early Friday as investors held back ahead of the release of the Fed’s key inflation metric, the PCE report, while global trade tensions added to the cautious mood.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Ulta Beauty (NASDAQ:ULTA) +8% – The stock climbed after the beauty retailer delivered upbeat Q1 results and lifted its full-year outlook, citing a strong start to FY2025. Ulta now projects FY2025 net sales between $11.5B and $11.7B, slightly widening its prior range of $11.5B–$11.6B and bracketing the Street's $11.59B estimate. Adjusted EPS is now forecast at $22.65 to $23.20, up from the earlier $22.50 to $22.90, and surrounding the $22.96 consensus. Comparable sales are expected to range from flat to +1.5%, compared to the earlier projection of flat to +1.0%.
  • UiPath (NYSE:PATH) +11% – Shares rallied after the company posted strong Q1 results and issued upbeat guidance for Q2 and the full year. For Q2, UiPath expects revenue between $345M and $350M, comfortably above the Street’s estimate of $331.82M. Annualized recurring revenue (ARR) is forecast in the range of $1.715B to $1.720B by the end of July, with non-GAAP operating income of around $40M. For the FY2026, revenue is projected at $1.549B to $1.554B, above the $1.52B consensus, with ARR targeted at $1.820B to $1.825B and non-GAAP operating income of roughly $305M.
  • Zscaler (NASDAQ:ZS) +4% – Shares gained after the cybersecurity firm posted strong FQ3 results, highlighted by a 23% Y/Y increase in revenue. Calculated billings rose 25%, and deferred revenue climbed 26%. The company also named Kevin Rubin as its new CFO. Looking ahead, Zscaler guided FQ4 revenue between $705M and $707M, just below the $707.96M consensus, and expects non-GAAP EPS of $0.79–$0.80, above $0.77 consensus. For FY2025, it forecast revenue of $2.659B–$2.661B vs. the consensus of $2.65B, billings of $3.184B–$3.189B, and EPS of $3.18–$3.19, surpassing the consensus of $3.08.

Biggest stock losers

  • The Gap (NYSE:GAP) -15% – Shares tumbled as investors reacted to the company’s FY2025 guidance, which notably excluded the potential impact of proposed tariffs. Management warned that tariffs could hit operating income by $100M–$150M even after mitigation efforts. Meanwhile, Q2 net sales are expected to remain flat Y/Y at $3.72B, slightly below the $3.73B consensus. For the full year, Gap forecasts 1–2% sales growth, or $15.25B–$15.40B, roughly in line with estimates, and operating income of $1.19B–$1.21B, up 8–10% Y/Y.
  • American Eagle Outfitters (NYSE:AEO) -7% – Shares declined after a mixed Q1 report. Total revenue fell 5% Y/Y to $1.1B, with comparable sales down 3%. The Aerie brand posted a 4% drop in comps, while American Eagle brand comps slipped 2%. Gross margin contracted sharply to 29.6% from 40.6% a year earlier. For Q2, the company expects a 5% decline in revenue and a 3% drop in comparable sales, with projected operating income of $40M–$45M.
  • Marvell Technology (NASDAQ:MRVL) -3% – Shares dipped despite the chipmaker posting Q1 results and forward guidance that exceeded analyst forecasts. For Q2, the company expects revenue of approximately $2B, plus or minus 5%, slightly above the $1.98B consensus. Adjusted EPS is projected at $0.67 (±$0.05), also ahead of the $0.66 analysts were expecting, suggesting continued strength in its end markets.

data.title: Stock index futures muted ahead of inflation gauge
data.description:
Stock index futures were little changed on Friday as investors awaited any more news related to President Trump's tariffs and also looked forward to key inflation gauge. 

S&P 500 futures (SPX) -0.2%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) -0.1%, and Dow futures (INDU) were unchanged.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 1 basis point to 4.44%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) was flat at 3.96%.

The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit on Thursday issued an administrative stay blocking a ruling from a lower court on Wednesday that struck down “reciprocal” tariffs Donald Trump imposed in April.

So even if the court ruling is upheld, it doesn’t look like the world is heading back to the pre-2025 tariff situation, even if there is a pathway to a world with less tariffs after the ruling, Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said.

Wall Street's major averages on Thursday ended with marginal gains amid a bond rally sparked by weak GDP data and more back-and-forth on tariffs.

“Risk assets initially surged yesterday after the surprise court ruling, but they gave up those gains later in the day as markets digested several alternative avenues they could pursue,” Reid said.

The Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge will be under the spotlight on Friday.  

The PCE price index is expected to tick up 0.1% M/M in April vs. flat in March, according to the consensus of economists. The core PCE price index is also expected to edge up 0.1% in April, vs. 0.0% in the prior month.

On a year-over-year basis, headline PCE prices are anticipated to rise 2.1%, slowing from the 2.3% rate in March, while core PCE is expected to hold at 2.6%.

“The U.S. April personal consumer expenditure deflator is due today. It is too early to see the direct effects of trade taxes,” UBS' Paul Donovan said.

The May consumer sentiment and the May Chicago PMI will land during market hours. The former is expected to come in at 50.8 and the latter at 45.1.

Final May Michigan consumer sentiment data says little of itself. However, comparisons to the preliminary data suggest the evolution of partisan bias as Trump retreated from trade taxes, Donovan added.

data.title: Synopsys suspends sales in China to comply with US export curbs - report
data.description:
Synopsys (NASDAQ:SNPS) has directed its employees in China to suspend services and sales in the country and stop taking new orders to adhere to new U.S. export curbs, Reuters reported citing an internal letter.

Products impacted include design software and chemicals for semiconductors, the report added.

Synopsys did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

On Thursday, only a day after Synopsys, released its second quarter results and guidance, the company suspended its guidance for the quarter in progress and full-year fiscal 2025 due to new export controls related to China. Synopsys said it received a letter from the Bureau of Industry and Security of the U.S. Department of Commerce informing the company of new export restrictions related to China.

Synopsys had said then that it was assessing the potential impact of the BIS Letter on its business, operating results and financial condition.

On Wednesday it was reported that the BIS was sending letters to electronic design automation, or EDA, companies directing them to stop selling hardware, software and services to China, in an effort to slow China's development of advanced semiconductors.

The internal letter sent to employees in China said, "based on our initial interpretation, these new restrictions broadly prohibit the sales of our products and services in China and are effective as of May 29, 2025," as per the report.

To comply, Synopsys said it was stopping sales and fulfillment in China and halting new orders until it gets further clarification, the report noted.

The actions affect all customers in China, including employees of global customers working at sites in China and Chinese military users wherever they are located, according to the letter.

In addition, the letter noted that the Chinese customers' access to the company's customer support portal SolvNetPlus has been disabled.

Other companies potentially affected by the new controls include Cadence Design Systems (CDNS), Keysight Technologies (KEYS) and Ansys (ANSS), which is currently in the process of being acquired by Synopsys.

Synopsys, Cadence and Siemens's Mentor Graphics control over 70% of China's EDA market, Chinese state news agency Xinhua reported in April.

Shares of Synopsys dipped about 0.5% premarket on Friday.

data.title: US dollar faces fifth monthly drop amid trade, fiscal uncertainty
data.description:
The U.S. dollar (DXY) wavered on Friday, heading toward its fifth consecutive monthly decline, as traders remained cautious amid ongoing uncertainty over trade policy and the nation’s fiscal outlook, while anticipating key inflation data due later in the day.

The US Dollar Index (DXY), which tracks the Greenback’s value against six major currencies, ticked up to 99.54 at the time of writing, but was on course for a 0.7% monthly decline. 

The greenback experienced a volatile week, closing lower in the previous session after a federal court temporarily reinstated President Donald Trump's broadest tariffs—just one day after a different court had issued an immediate block on them.

"What weighed on the dollar more yesterday seemed to be the US macro data," ING analysts said.

Personal consumption got revised down to 1.2% from 1.7% quarter-on-quarter annualised in the first quarter GDP release.

"And a pick-up in initial claims didn't help either. In effect, we saw a return to traditional correlations, where US Treasury yields dropped 5bp and the dollar weakened," Chris Turner of ING wrote.

Meanwhile, Jerome Powell, the chair of the Federal Reserve, held his first one-on-one meeting with President Donald Trump since the beginning of his first term in office.  

Elsewhere, the euro was slightly lower at $1.1331, ahead of German inflation data for May, while the Swiss franc was little changed at 0.8243 per dollar.

data.title: BP expands board with veteran Dave Hager as non-executive director
data.description:
• BP (NYSE:BP) appointed David Hager—a veteran of the U.S. oil and gas industry, as a non-executive director effective June 2, 2025.
  • Hager has over 40 years of experience in the sector and served as CEO of Devon Energy from 2015 to 2021 and as executive chair from 2021 to 2023.
  • The appointment comes at a time when BP (NYSE:BP) is shifting its strategy to increase hydrocarbon production, aiming to more than double its oil and gas output in the U.S. by the end of the decade. 
  • The move aligns with the company's broader strategic reset, which includes cutting spending on low-carbon investments and focusing more on oil and gas production.
  • "On behalf of the board, I am delighted to welcome Dave to bp. He brings deep-rooted knowledge of the US upstream oil and gas industry. His strategic focus combined with his experience in driving operational results and capital efficiency will be an asset to the board." said Helge Lund, chair of BP.
